dn11:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying at Nālandā in Pāvārika’s mango grove.
dn11:1.3: Then Kevaṭṭa the householder approached the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down, sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to the Blessed One:
dn11:1.4: “Lord, this Nālandā is powerful, both prosperous and populous, filled with people who have faith in the Blessed One.
dn11:1.5: It would be good if the Blessed One were to direct a monk to display a miracle of psychic power from his superior human state
dn11:1.6: so that Nālandā would to an even greater extent have faith in the Blessed One.”
dn11:1.7: When this was said, the Blessed One said to Kevaṭṭa the householder,
dn11:1.8: “Kevaṭṭa, I don’t teach the monks in this way:
dn11:1.9: ‘Come, monks, display a miracle of psychic power to the lay people clad in white.’”
dn11:3.1: A third time, Kevaṭṭa the householder said to the Blessed One:
dn11:3.2: “I won’t argue with the Blessed One,
dn11:3.4: Lord, this Nālandā is powerful, both prosperous and populous, filled with people who have faith in the Blessed One.
dn11:3.5: It would be good if the Blessed One were to direct a monk to display a miracle of psychic power from his superior human state
dn11:3.6: so that Nālandā would to an even greater extent have faith in the Blessed One.” A third time, the Blessed One said to Kevaṭṭa the householder, “Kevaṭṭa, I don’t teach the monks in this way: ‘Come, monks, display a miracle of psychic power to the lay people clad in white.’
dn11:3.8: “Kevaṭṭa, there are these three miracles that I have declared, having directly known and realized them for myself.
dn11:3.10: The miracle of psychic power, the miracle of telepathy, and the miracle of instruction.
dn11:4.1: “And what is the miracle of psychic power?
dn11:4.2: There is the case where a monk wields manifold psychic powers. Having been one he becomes many; having been many he becomes one. He appears. He vanishes. He goes unimpeded through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space. He dives in and out of the earth as if it were water. He walks on water without sinking as if it were dry land. Sitting cross-legged he flies through the air like a winged bird. With his hand he touches and strokes even the sun and moon, so mighty and powerful. He exercises influence with his body even as far as the Brahmā worlds.
dn11:4.3: “Then someone who has faith and conviction in him sees him wielding manifold psychic powers… exercising influence with his body even as far as the Brahmā worlds.
dn11:4.4: He reports this to someone who has no faith and no conviction, telling him,
dn11:4.5: ‘Isn’t it awesome. Isn’t it astounding, how great the power, how great the prowess of this contemplative.
dn11:4.6: Just now I saw him wielding manifold psychic powers… exercising influence with his body even as far as the Brahmā worlds.’
dn11:5.1: Then the person without faith, without conviction, would say to the person with faith and with conviction:
dn11:5.2: ‘Sir, there is a charm called the Gandhāri charm
dn11:5.3: by which the monk wielded manifold psychic powers… exercising influence with his body even as far as the Brahmā worlds.’
dn11:5.5: Isn’t that what the man without faith, without conviction, would say to the man with faith and with conviction?”
dn11:5.6: “Yes, lord, that’s just what he would say.”
dn11:5.7: “Seeing this drawback to the miracle of psychic power, Kevaṭṭa, I feel ashamed, repelled, and disgusted with the miracle of psychic power.
dn11:6.1: “And what is the miracle of telepathy?
dn11:6.2: There is the case where a monk reads the minds, the mental events, the thoughts, the ponderings of other beings, other individuals, (saying,)
dn11:6.3: ‘Such is your thinking, here is where your thinking is, thus is your mind.’
dn11:6.4: “Then someone who has faith and conviction in him sees him reading the minds… of other beings.…
dn11:6.6: He reports this to someone who has no faith and no conviction, telling him,
dn11:6.7: ‘Isn’t it awesome. Isn’t it astounding, how great the power, how great the prowess of this contemplative.
dn11:6.8: Just now I saw him reading the minds… of other beings.…’
dn11:7.1: Then the person without faith, without conviction, would say to the person with faith and with conviction:
dn11:7.2: ‘Sir, there is a charm called the Maṇikā charm by which the monk read the minds… of other beings.…’
dn11:7.6: Isn’t that what the man without faith, without conviction, would say to the man with faith and with conviction?”
dn11:7.7: “Yes, lord, that’s just what he would say.”
dn11:7.8: “Seeing this drawback to the miracle of telepathy, Kevaṭṭa, I feel horrified, humiliated, and disgusted with the miracle of telepathy.
dn11:8.1: “And what is the miracle of instruction?
dn11:8.2: There is the case where a monk gives instruction in this way:
dn11:8.3: ‘Direct your thought in this way, don’t direct it in that. Attend to things in this way, don’t attend to them in that. Let go of this, enter and remain in that.’
dn11:8.4: This, Kevaṭṭa, is called the miracle of instruction.
dn11:9-66.1: “Then there is the case where a Tathāgata appears in the world, worthy and rightly self-awakened...
dn11:9-66.2: “When he has thus gone forth, he lives restrained by the rules of the monastic code...
dn11:9-66.3: he enters and remains in the first jhāna...
dn11:9-66.4: “This, too, is called the miracle of instruction...
dn11:9-66.5: he enters and remains in the second jhāna...
dn11:9-66.6: He enters and remains in the third jhāna...
dn11:9-66.7: he enters and remains in the fourth jhāna...
dn11:9-66.8: “This, too, is called the miracle of instruction.
dn11:9-66.9: Insight Knowledge, etc. “With his mind thus concentrated, purified, & bright, unblemished, free from defects, pliant, malleable, steady, & attained to imperturbability, he directs and inclines it to knowledge & vision… to creating a mind-made body… to the modes of supranormal powers… to the divine ear-element… to knowledge of the awareness of other beings… to knowledge of the recollection of past lives… to knowledge of the passing away & re-appearance of beings… to the knowledge of the ending of effluents. He discerns, as it has come to be, that ‘This is stress… This is the origination of stress… This is the cessation of stress… This is the way leading to the cessation of stress… These are effluents… This is the origination of effluents…
dn11:9-66.10: This, too, is called the miracle of instruction...
dn11:9-66.11: There is nothing further for this world.’
dn11:9-66.12: This, too, is called the miracle of instruction.
dn11:67.1: “These are the three miracles that I declare, Kevaṭṭa, having directly known and realized them for myself.
dn11:67.3: “Once, Kevaṭṭa, this train of thought arose in the awareness of a certain monk in this very Saṅgha of monks:
dn11:67.4: ‘Where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?’
dn11:68.1: Then he attained to such a state of concentration that the way leading to the devas appeared in his concentrated mind.
dn11:68.2: So he approached the Devas of the Retinue of the Four Great Kings and, on arrival, asked them,
dn11:68.3: ‘Friends, where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?’
dn11:68.4: “When this was said, the Devas of the Retinue of the Four Great Kings said to the monk,
dn11:68.6: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:68.7: But there are the Four Great Kings who are higher and more sublime than we.
dn11:68.9: where the four great elements… cease without trace.’
dn11:69.1: “So the monk approached the Four Great Kings and, on arrival, asked them,
dn11:69.2: ‘Friends, where do these four great elements… cease without trace?’
dn11:69.3: “When this was said, the Four Great Kings said to the monk,
dn11:69.5: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:69.6: But there are the Devas of the Thirty-three who are higher and more sublime than we.
dn11:70.1: “So the monk approached the Devas of the Thirty-three and, on arrival, asked them,
dn11:70.2: ‘Friends, where do these four great elements… cease without trace?’
dn11:70.3: “When this was said, the Devas of the Thirty-three said to the monk,
dn11:70.5: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:70.6: But there is Sakka, the ruler of the devas, who is higher and more sublime than we.
dn11:70.8: “So the monk approached Sakka, the ruler of the devas, and, on arrival, asked him,
dn11:71.1: ‘Friend, where do these four great elements… cease without trace?’
dn11:71.2: “When this was said, Sakka, the ruler of the devas, said to the monk,
dn11:71.4: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:72.1: But there are the Yāma devas [Devas of the Hours] who are higher and more sublime than I. They should know.…’.… “The Yāma devas said, ‘We also don’t know.…
dn11:73.1: But there is the deva named Suyāma.… He should know.… ’ .… “Suyāma said, ‘I also don’t know.…
dn11:74.1: But there are the Tusita devas.…  They should know.… ’.… “The Tusita devas said, ‘We also don’t know.…
dn11:75.1: But there is the deva named Santusita.… He should know.… ’.… “Santusita said, ‘I also don’t know.…
dn11:76.1: But there are the Nimmānaratī devas.… They should know.… ’ .… “The Nimmānaratī devas [devas who delight in creation] said, ‘We also don’t know.…
dn11:77.1: But there is the deva named Sunimmita.… He should know.… ’ .… “Sunimmita said, ‘I also don’t know.…
dn11:77.2: But there are the Paranimmitavasavattī devas [Devas Wielding control over the creations of others].… They should know.… ’ .… “The Paranimmitavasavattī devas said, ‘We also don’t know.…
dn11:78.1: But there is the deva named Vasavatti.…
dn11:79.1: “So the monk approached the deva Vassavatti and, on arrival, asked him,
dn11:79.2: ‘Friend, where do these four great elements… cease without trace?’
dn11:79.3: “When this was said, the deva Vassavatti said to the monk,
dn11:79.5: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:79.6: But there are the Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue who are higher and more sublime than I.
dn11:79.8: where the four great elements… cease without trace’.…
dn11:80.1: “Then the monk attained to such a state of concentration that the way leading to the Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue appeared in his concentrated mind.
dn11:80.2: So he approached the Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue and, on arrival, asked them,
dn11:80.3: ‘Friends, where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?’
dn11:80.4: “When this was said, the Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue said to the monk,
dn11:80.6: where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:80.7: But there is Brahmā, the Great Brahmā, the Conqueror, the Unconquered, the All-Seeing, All-Powerful, the Sovereign Lord, the Maker, Creator, Chief, Appointer, and Ruler, Father of All That Have Been and Shall Be. He is higher and more sublime than we.
dn11:80.9: where the four great elements… cease without trace.’
dn11:80.10: “‘But where, friends, is the Great Brahmā now?’
dn11:80.11: “‘Monk, we also don’t know where Brahmā is or in what way Brahmā is.
dn11:80.12: But when signs appear, light shines forth, and a radiance appears, Brahmā will appear. For these are the portents of Brahmā’s appearance: Light shines forth and a radiance appears.’
dn11:81.1: “Then it was not long before Brahmā appeared.
dn11:81.2: “So the monk approached the Great Brahmā and, on arrival, said,
dn11:81.3: ‘Friend, where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?’
dn11:81.4: “When this was said, the Great Brahmā said to the monk,
dn11:81.5: ‘I, monk, am Brahmā, the Great Brahmā, the Conqueror, the Unconquered, the All-Seeing, All-Powerful, the Sovereign Lord, the Maker, Creator, Chief, Appointer, and Ruler, Father of All That Have Been and Shall Be.’
dn11:82.1: A second time, the monk said to the Great Brahmā,
dn11:82.2: ‘Friend, I didn’t ask you if you were
dn11:82.3: Brahmā, the Great Brahmā, the Conqueror, the Unconquered, the All-Seeing, All-Powerful, the Sovereign Lord, the Maker, Creator, Chief, Appointer, and Ruler, Father of All That Have Been and Shall Be.
dn11:82.5: where these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace.’
dn11:82.6: “A second time, the Great Brahmā said to the monk,
dn11:82.7: ‘I, monk, am Brahmā, the Great Brahmā, the Conqueror, the Unconquered, the All-Seeing, All-Powerful, the Sovereign Lord, the Maker, Creator, Chief, Appointer, and Ruler, Father of All That Have Been and Shall Be.’
dn11:83.1: “A third time, the monk said to the Great Brahmā,
dn11:83.2: ‘Friend, I didn’t ask you if you were
dn11:83.3: Brahmā, the Great Brahmā, the Conqueror, the Unconquered, the All-Seeing, All-Powerful, the Sovereign Lord, the Maker, Creator, Chief, Appointer, and Ruler, Father of All That Have Been and Shall Be.
dn11:83.5: where these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace.’
dn11:83.6: “Then the Great Brahmā, taking the monk by the arm and leading him off to one side, said to him,
dn11:83.7: ‘These Devas of the Retinue of Brahmā believe, “There is nothing that the Great Brahmā does not know. There is nothing that the Great Brahmā does not see. There is nothing of which the Great Brahmā is unaware. There is nothing that the Great Brahmā has not realized.”
dn11:83.8: That is why I did not say in their presence that
dn11:83.9: I, too, don’t know where the four great elements… cease without trace.
dn11:83.10: So it’s your own wrongdoing, your own mistake, in that—bypassing the Blessed One—you searched outside for an answer to this question.
dn11:83.11: Go right back to the Blessed One and, on arrival, ask him this question. However he answers you, that’s how you should remember it.’
dn11:84.1: “Then—just as a strong man might extend his flexed arm or flex his extended arm—the monk disappeared from the Brahmā world and immediately appeared in front of me.
dn11:84.2: Having bowed down to me, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to me,
dn11:84.3: ‘Lord, where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?’
dn11:85.1: “When this was said, I said to him,
dn11:85.2: ‘Once, monk, some sea-faring merchants, taking a shore-sighting bird, set out across the ocean in their ship.
dn11:85.3: When they could not see the shore from their ship, they released the shore-sighting bird.
dn11:85.4: It flew to the east; it flew to the south; it flew to the west; it flew to the north; it flew to straight up; it flew to the intermediate directions.
dn11:85.5: If it saw the shore in any direction, it flew there and was gone.
dn11:85.6: If it did not see the shore in any direction, it returned right back to the ship.
dn11:85.7: In the same way, monk, having searched as far as the Brahmā world and not receiving an answer to this question, you have come right back to my presence.
dn11:85.8: “‘This question should not be asked in this way:
dn11:85.9: Where do these four great elements—the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, and the wind property—cease without trace?
dn11:85.10: Instead, the question should be asked like this:
dn11:85.16: brought to a stop without trace?
dn11:85.25: are all brought to a stop without trace.
dn11:85.26: With the cessation of (the aggregate of) consciousness
dn11:85.27: each is here brought to a stop.’”
dn11:85.28: That is what the Blessed One said.
dn11:85.29: Gratified, Kevaṭṭa the householder delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
